title: Tomato soup
subtitle: Roasted vine tomatoes, slow-cooked down with onion and stock.
excerpt: Vine tomatoes roasted until their skins blister, then blended with softened onion, garlic, and vegetable stock into a smooth, deeply flavoured soup. Ten minutes of prep, an hour in the oven and on the hob. Good with crusty bread.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), includes a tomato soup recipe (entry 133) using fresh tomatoes, onion, and veal stock. Project Gutenberg #10136. The modern vegetarian version substitutes vegetable stock and extends the cook with a roasting stage that Beeton did not use but which became standard in British home cooking through the late twentieth century.

content.text: Tomato soup made properly starts in a hot oven, not on the hob. Roasting concentrates the tomatoes, caramelises their edges, and drives off the thin watery liquid that makes raw-blended versions taste flat. The result is a deep, rounded soup that needs no cream to feel substantial.

Use vine tomatoes when you can: they carry more flavour than loose ones sold on the vine. Out of season, a mixture of vine tomatoes and a tin of good-quality plum tomatoes works well. The tin adds body; the fresh ones add the roasted edge.
This is a beginner recipe. The only technique is patience: roasting takes forty minutes and you do nothing during it. Total work time is under fifteen minutes.
You need a large roasting tin to hold the tomatoes cut-side up in a single layer, a large saucepan, and a stick blender. A jug blender works but creates more washing up.

Heat the oven to 200 °C fan. Arrange {{tomato}} halved tomatoes cut-side up in a large roasting tin. Scatter the onion pieces and {{garlic}} of unpeeled garlic cloves around them. Drizzle with {{olive-oil}} of olive oil and season with {{sea-salt-flakes}} of sea salt and {{black-pepper}} of pepper. Roast for 40 minutes until the tomato edges are slightly charred and collapsing.

Tip the roasted tomatoes and onion into a large saucepan, scraping in all the juices. Squeeze the garlic cloves from their skins and add the soft flesh; discard the skins. Add {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée and {{caster-sugar}} of sugar. Pour in {{stock-vegetable}} of hot vegetable stock. Bring to a gentle simmer and cook for 10 minutes.

Remove from the heat. Use a stick blender to blend until smooth. Taste and adjust seasoning. If the soup is thicker than you like, stir in a little more stock. Ladle into bowls and finish with fresh basil leaves and a swirl of double cream if using.

items.fix: Return to the oven for another 10 to 15 minutes. Add a second teaspoon of tomato purée and a small pinch more sugar to compensate.
items.cause: Tomatoes did not roast long enough, or were very watery out-of-season tomatoes.

items.fix: Stir in hot water or stock a splash at a time until the consistency is right.
Add another half teaspoon of sugar and stir well. A pinch of bicarbonate of soda also neutralises acidity without sweetening.
items.cause: Tomatoes are naturally high in acidity, amplified by roasting.

items.fix: Pass through a sieve, pressing with the back of a ladle. Most stick blenders need 2 to 3 minutes of blending to achieve a truly smooth result.
items.cause: Tomato skin not fully broken down by the stick blender.
items.symptom: Stringy or grainy texture after blending

content.text: Tomato soup keeps well. Refrigerate for up to three days; the flavour improves overnight. Freeze in labelled portions for up to three months. Add any cream garnish after reheating rather than before freezing.

Tomato soup has a particular place in British home cooking. It is both the tinned soup of childhood and the recipe home cooks come back to once they taste a fresh version. The Victorian household manual by Mrs Beeton has a tomato soup, made with veal stock and fresh tomatoes from the kitchen garden. The move to vegetable stock and the roasting stage came later. Part of it was the British allotment tradition of using up a summer glut. The roasted version is now the standard home recipe.
